source_label: Murray N. Rothbard — What Has Government Done to Our Money?
excerpt: The history of money has been the history of government interference with that money. Every state-managed currency has eventually been inflated; not as an accident, but as the working-out of the underlying incentive structure.
body:
Source excerpt:
The history of money has been the history of government interference with that money. Every state-managed currency has eventually been inflated; not as an accident, but as the working-out of the underlying incentive structure.

---

Cited by leaf "Fiat dilution and the historical record":
The historical record of state-managed currencies is unambiguous: every fiat regime in recorded history has been inflated, often catastrophically, eventually [1]. Rothbard catalogues the pattern from the Roman denarius (debased steadily from ~95% silver under Augustus to under 5% by the third century, contributing materially to the empire's monetary collapse) through medieval coin clipping, John Law's eighteenth-century paper experiment in France, the American Continentals (which gave the language the phrase 'not worth a Continental'), the Weimar mark, the Hungarian pengő (the worst hyperinflation in recorded history, with prices doubling every fifteen hours at peak), Zimbabwe, Venezuela, and dozens more [1].

The pattern is not random. It follows from the structure of the situation: any institution with the unilateral power to create money at zero marginal cost faces a constant temptation to use that power, and that temptation gets exercised under fiscal stress. Hülsmann argues this in *The Ethics of Money Production* — debasement is not a regrettable lapse in monetary policy, it is the predictable equilibrium of any monopoly issuer, because the *option* to debase is itself a fiscal asset that politicians cannot afford to leave unexercised forever [2].

The modern post-1971 experiment with floating fiat money is, on this reading, just the most recent iteration of a very old pattern. The dollar has lost roughly 95% of its purchasing power against a basket of consumer goods since the founding of the Federal Reserve in 1913, and the pace has accelerated since the closing of the gold window. The same is broadly true of every other major fiat currency over the same window [3]. None of this is to predict imminent collapse — fiat regimes can sustain themselves for decades or centuries; the Roman denarius took two hundred years to fully unwind. The claim is structural: the *direction* is monotonic, even if the speed varies.

What Bitcoin offers is the first credibly-bound monetary system in history that is not subject to this dynamic — because there is no issuer with the option to debase. The supply schedule is enforced by every node, and changing it would require coordinated convincing of the operators themselves, who have no fiscal incentive to inflate.

---

Cited by leaf "What is sound money":
In the Austrian tradition, *sound money* is money that emerges from the market rather than being decreed by an authority. Carl Menger's account is the canonical one: people independently converge on whichever good has the best monetary properties — durability, divisibility, portability, recognisability, and above all difficulty of production — until that good becomes the medium of exchange [1]. Sound money is therefore a discovered institution, not a designed one.

Mises sharpens the definition in *The Theory of Money and Credit*: sound money is money whose supply is determined by the cost of producing it, not by the will of a central issuer [2]. Gold qualified historically because mining new gold to debase the stock was expensive enough that the supply grew slowly and predictably. A government IOU does not qualify, because the marginal cost of issuing one more unit is essentially zero — and what costs nothing to produce will eventually be produced in quantity [3].

The practical claim that follows is straightforward: when the issuer of money can create more of it without effort, they will, and the people closest to the spigot benefit at everyone else's expense. Rothbard argues at book length that every fiat regime in recorded history has followed this pattern — the temptation is structural, not personal [3]. Sound money removes the temptation by removing the discretion.

Bitcoin's claim to this category rests on a different mechanism: the supply schedule is enforced by every node, not by the cost of mining. New coins are produced by miners, but how many are produced and on what schedule is fixed in software that thousands of independent operators run and verify against. That is a stronger guarantee than gold's — gold's scarcity could in principle be overturned by an asteroid mining breakthrough; bitcoin's cannot be overturned without coordinated convincing of the operators themselves to change the rules. In Austrian terms, this places it firmly in the sound-money category that gold previously occupied alone.
